original_abstract: Mixed nickel–iron oxyhydroxides (Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH) are among the most efficient catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in alkaline media, yet the atomistic origin of their high activity—and whether Ni or Fe serves as the active center—remains a subject of debate. In this work, we use operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy with high energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD-XAS) combined with density functional theory (DFT+U) to investigate the local structure and electronic states of (Ni,Fe)OOH across a wide composition range. Our results show that at iron concentrations below ~25%, Fe³⁺ ions substitute for Ni³⁺/⁴⁺ in the γ-NiOOH lattice. In this environment, the Fe–O bond lengths are significantly compressed (~1.90 Å) compared to pure iron oxyhydroxide, a distortion imposed by the edge-sharing [NiO₆] octahedral framework. As the iron content increases further, a phase-separated, less active γ-FeOOH component emerges. Theoretical calculations demonstrate that these substituted Fe sites possess near-optimal adsorption energies for OER intermediates, explaining the observed 500-fold enhancement in catalytic activity compared to pure Ni and Fe parent phases. Conversely, Ni sites remain substantially less active. These findings identify Fe as the true active site in (Ni,Fe)OOH and highlight how the host lattice can tune a dopant's local environment to reach peak electrochemical performance.
humanized_abstract: Mixed nickel–iron oxyhydroxides (Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH) are known to be one of the most active catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in alkaline solution, but the reasons of their high performance and which element between nickel and iron acts as the active centre are still unclear. Using operando HERFD-XAS (High Energy Resolution Fluorescence Detection - X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy) combined with DFT+U (Density Functional Theory +U) calculations, we evidenced the local structure and electronic state of nickel and iron species in a wide composition range of mixed nickel–iron oxyhydroxides. We observed that iron is present at low concentrations (below ~25% w/w) occupies the Ni3+/4+ site of the γ-NiOOH structure as Fe3+ ions with a shortened Fe-O bond length of about 1.90 Å, probably as a consequence of the compressive forces imposed by the edge-sharing [NiO₆] structure. At higher iron concentrations, a non-active phase of γ-FeOOH is present. Density functional theory calculations revealed that the Fe³⁺ ions in the (Ni,Fe)OOH structure have near-optimal adsorption energy values for the OER intermediates, explaining the 500 times higher activity with respect to pure nickel and iron oxides. Instead, Ni sites are less active. Therefore, Fe is the active species in (Ni,Fe)OOH catalysts and its activity is optimized in the host structure that adjusts the Fe local environment to obtain the highest electrocatalytic performance.

We observed that iron is present at low concentrations (below ~25% w/w) occupies the Ni3+/4+ site of the γ-NiOOH structure as Fe3+ ions with a shortened Fe-O bond length of about 1.90 Å, probably as a consequence of the compressive forces imposed by the edge-sharing [NiO₆] structure. Density functional theory calculations revealed that the Fe³⁺ ions in the (Ni,Fe)OOH structure have near-optimal adsorption energy values for the OER intermediates, explaining the 500 times higher activity with respect to pure nickel and iron oxides.
